Attending site to check Dissolved O2 and Ammonium sensors (W11364718). Found Tank#2 ammonium reading was above full scale. Operations does not use these probes to control any part of the process. It is unclear if this high reading caused an error in the D.O./Ammonium transmitter. I adjusted the ammonium value from above 10.25, to 8.3mg/L, in case this was causing and issue with the controller. Review alarms on local D.O./Ammonium controller - the were many alarms related to the Tank#2 ammonium reading registering above full-scale. All alarms were acknowledged. Alarms did not return after 10 minutes. Notified Neil Ott and Kevin Kerek.

Under the direction of Kevin Kerek, modified the PAC program for Raw Sewage Pump 4.
When pump 4 is called to run on duty 1 in auto, pump 4 will run 100 % for 2 min to remove any clogging. 
After 2 minutes timer, if pump 4 flow meter has low flow alarm or pump efficiency is low then keep running pump 4 to 100% speed until it generates flow.
If there is no flow or efficiency alarm after the start up then, continue running pump at calculated speed based on Level.

LDTEXT:

-was able to back flush line 1 and clear debris
-line 2 valve was not able top be cleared, and over torque alarms is outstanding, will notify team and create WO
-new flushing system is too small (not enough flow), pressure is okay, but not enough volume, it should be located upstream of the valve to clear valve of debris, and likely doesn?t have the volume to clear anything heavy or jammed. It is capable of clearing smaller debris in line possibly.
-need new valves that don?t get clogged, and a larger flushing system
-has been multiple times we have been down to one line in the last few weeks and this system is brand new
-need 4? flushing lines similar to tipping buckets used in AUEQSJ
-seems every few days there is a clog/overtorque if not multiple clogs every day

- Test ran generator 1 and 2 starting from 16:26 to test UV function during generator test
- Placed UV 1 and 2 into local on 
- Known issue but testing again to confirm. Testing revealed that after ATS power transfer for generator 1, generator 1 will shut off UV1 because generator 1 powers UV1. After ATS power transfer for generator 2, generator 2 will shut off UV2 because generator 2 powers UV2. 
- Due to this, during power transfers or monthly generator test, if testing generator 1, UV 2 should be running in duty to prevent UV1 from shutting off. The reverse for generator 2, for generator 2, UV1 should be running in duty to prevent UV2 from shutting off. This will ensure UV dosage is applied at all times. Notified team, supervisor and TL.
- In addition, during power transfers UV channel 2 Bank A tends to fault due to power loss, causing "UV channel 2 Bank A power loss fault alarm". This tends to happen more frequently with generator 2 ATS. Will create a work order to address power loss fault for UV2 Bank A
- Due to this work, multiple UV and process alarms were created. Such as, UV channel 2 bank 1a power loss fault, sec clarifier 1 and 2 overload alarm, soda ash conveyor e stop alarm, uv channel 2 bank 1a major alarm, uv channel 2 common major alarm, uv system 2 warning alarm, Site hydro power failure alarm, aut transfer switch e pow, mechanical screen lockout alarm, disel gen run alarm, mechanical screen gen alarm. Trojan contractor was servicing UV Channel 2 Bank A and B for semiannual maintenance, and replaced the low level probe. The new probe was placed in but it ended up causing low level fault and level signal fault on UV2. He determined that it was the relay not functioning properly and not sending the signal back to the HMI. He tested this theory by removing the probe and trying to trigger a low level fault, which did not show alarms on HMI. He went further later on and wanted to test UV2's relay by using UV1's relay. Contractor removed the relays from UV1 bank a and b while UV1 was active, in duty 1 and running. Hence, UV1 shut off immediately due to the relay being removed and triggered a low level fault on UV1 and no UV was dosed from 15:12 ~ 15:13 with average flow of 12.5 L/s. Roughly 0.75 m^3 of final effluent was bypassed. The bypass could not be contained due to the large volume moving through and no clean up was needed.

On site at 0830 to investigate the generator low fuel alarm. Before starting called ROC to infor them that we will be causing alarms from our work at 0900. We placed generator into manual and disconnected battery. We found that the low fuel float was not correctly set at a proper height and when the fuel gets to 60% in the tank it causes nuisance alarms for a gen warning. We for the time being bypassed the float leaving the low low that will trip the generator into a fault at 20% low fuel. When complete we placed the get back to auto and reconnected battery at 1000. We test ran to confirm operation and cleared alarms. Under direction of oic.

ROC called @05:25 for CT not achieved alarm, on well startup:  Requested ROC to leave wells in man/off until morning. 
- Reviewed trends @07:45, found that the wells had started @05:17 and interlocked @05:20.  Before the wells started, the effluent free chlorine was reading 0.55 mg/L
- The effluent free of 0.55 mg/L was pulled back from distrubtion during the time that the wells were off, as it was reading 0.55 mg/L before the wells activated.  
-  When the wells started up, and they got up to combined flow of ~ 40 L/s, the effluent cl2 of 0.55 mg/L was not enough to meet CT achieved.  The effluent reqired at the time was 0.57 mg/L.  However, it was water that was already treated, not water that had just came through the CT tank.  CT had previously been achieved on distribution water.  And as the wells were only running for 3 minutes, it was not enough time for raw water to go through the CT tank.  Used the virtual CT calculator to confirm.
- Cleared alarms @08:00 
- @08:15: Started well #2 in manual, left well #1 in man/off until water from well #2 has cleared the CT tank.
